title: Artificial intelligence tools expand scientists' impact but contract science's focus

data_scope.ai_eras_covered: multiple distinct eras of AI (pre-deep-learning through large-model era)

core_conclusion: Analyzing 41.3 million natural-science papers, AI-augmented researchers publish 3.02x more papers, receive 4.84x more citations, and become project leaders 1.37 years earlier — yet AI adoption collectively shrinks scientific topic volume by 4.63% and reduces inter-scientist engagement by 22%. AI tools automate established, data-rich fields rather than exploring new frontiers, creating a paradox of individual gain versus collective scientific narrowing.
why_it_matters: First large-scale empirical quantification of AI's systemic effects on science; reveals structural bias toward data-rich fields; documents career consequences including junior researcher displacement; identifies Matthew effect in AI-paper citations; provides evidence base for AI governance in science funding.
why_nature: High-stakes societal question at a decisive moment for AI policy; unprecedented scale (41.3M papers) with rigorous BERT-based classification (F1=0.875); novel paradox framing (individual gain vs. collective cost) matches Nature's interdisciplinary mandate; bridges sociology, information science, and AI policy.
added_value_to_my_research: Geography of AI-driven knowledge contraction -> MGWR model of local contraction rate by infrastructure/GDP, analogous to digital-divide work
Hazard model for AI adoption decision -> mirrors EV-charging adoption diffusion, predicts GeoAI capacity growth by city/institution
RAG evaluation harness using SPECTER knowledge-extent metric -> benchmark for whether geospatial foundation model fine-tuning broadens or narrows the toolkit
Author-contribution task decomposition -> agent-based model of scientific community simulating AI-displacement of specific roles and emergent topic diversity
Sub-field replication for GeoAI/urban analytics -> self-diagnostic revealing whether spatial AI is converging on narrow prediction tasks vs. causal/equity questions

figures.caption: Increasing prevalence of AI adoption in science — tracks growth of AI-augmented research across disciplines and eras
AI enlarges paper impact and enhances researcher careers — 3.02x publication rate, 4.84x citations, earlier career-role transitions
AI adoption contracts knowledge extent — embedding-space analysis showing 4.63% shrinkage in collective topic volume
Reduced follow-on engagement and more overlapping works — 22% less inter-scientist engagement, higher intra-cluster redundancy in AI papers
